text: The surgeon uses diathermy to make superficial markings on the mucosal tissue, indicating the precise sites for removal and preservation, known as mucosal bridges.

The surgeon injects a solution of adrenaline dissolved in saline into the surgical site, aiming to reduce bleeding and facilitate precise anatomical dissection. This allows for clear identification of the internal and external sphincter muscles.
The surgeon makes a V-shaped incision at the mucocutaneous border and carefully dissects the hemorrhoidal pile using diathermy, extending the dissection up to two centimeters above the dentate line.
Following the excision of the first pile, the bright white internal sphincter is clearly visible, confirming the accurate dissection performed with the monopolar scalpel.
The surgeon repeats the V-shaped incision and careful dissection using diathermy on the remaining two piles, ensuring the internal sphincter remains visible and intact.
The surgeon uses an Eisenhammer anal retractor to achieve clear visualization of the internal sphincter throughout the operation.
The surgeon removes a residual skin tag and checks the integrity of the mucocutaneous bridges, ensuring no damage has occurred during the procedure.
